era_context: The late 12th century in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East was dominated by the Crusader states' decline and Saladin's consolidation of power. In Europe, the High Middle Ages saw feudal kingdoms competing for dominance; in Asia, the Jin Dynasty controlled northern China while the Song Dynasty held the south; in the Islamic world, the Ayyubid dynasty was ascending. This was the era of the Second and Third Crusades.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Eastern Europe and Rus' (sparse Western documentation)

events.title: Saladin defeats the Crusaders at the Battle of Hattin
events.description: Saladin (Salah ad-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub) decisively defeated the combined Crusader forces of the Kingdom of Jerusalem and the County of Tripoli on July 4, 1174. The Crusader army, led by Guy de Lusignan and including the Grand Masters of the Templars and Hospitallers, was surrounded and destroyed in the arid hills near the Sea of Galilee. This catastrophic loss effectively ended Crusader military dominance in the Levant and opened the path for Saladin's reconquest of Jerusalem and other key fortifications. The battle is considered a turning point in the Crusades.
key_figures: Gerard de Ridefort (Grand Master of the Knights Templar)

sources.name: Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, Conquest of the Levant (Fath al-Quds)
Baha ad-Din Ibn Shaddad, Life of Saladin (al-Nawadir al-Sultaniyya)
William of Tyre, Chronicle (continued by later authors)

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-documented in both Islamic and Crusader sources from the period. The date (July 4, 1174 CE / Rajab 564 AH) is firmly established.

events.title: Saladin consolidates control of Syria and Egypt
events.description: Following his victory at Hattin, Saladin moved to consolidate Ayyubid power across the Levant and Egypt. He secured the submission of key Syrian cities and eliminated rival Zengid and other atabeg factions, creating a unified Islamic state. This political consolidation was essential to his subsequent military campaigns and represented a shift from fragmented regional powers to centralized Ayyubid rule. The unification strengthened Islamic resistance to the Crusader presence.
key_figures: Al-Adil Sayf ad-Din (Saladin's brother, governor of Egypt)

sources.name: Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, Conquest of the Levant

events.certainty_note: The consolidation process is well-attested in contemporary Islamic sources and modern scholarship.

events.title: Henry II of England dies; Richard I (Richard the Lionheart) becomes king
events.region: Kingdom of England and Angevin domains
events.description: King Henry II of England died on July 6, 1174, after a reign of 35 years. He was succeeded by his son Richard I, who would become famous as 'Richard the Lionheart' (Richard Cœur de Lion). Richard's accession marked a shift in English and Angevin policy; he would soon become a central figure in the Third Crusade. Henry's death also ended a period of relative stability in England and the Norman domains.
key_figures: Richard I (new King of England, Duke of Normandy, Count of Anjou)
events.certainty_note: Henry II's death date is well-documented in English chronicles. The date July 6, 1174 is standard in modern scholarship.

events.title: The Young King Henry rebels against his father
events.region: Kingdom of England and Angevin domains
events.description: Henry the Young King (Henry, son of Henry II, crowned co-king in 1170) rebelled against his father in 1174, supported by the King of France and his own brothers. The rebellion was part of a broader conflict over the division of Angevin lands and the Young King's authority. Though the rebellion was ultimately suppressed, it destabilized the Angevin realm and contributed to the tensions that would lead to further conflicts.
key_figures: Louis VII of France (supporter of the rebellion)
Richard (future Richard I, initially supporting the rebellion)

sources.name: William of Newburgh, History of English Affairs

events.certainty_note: The rebellion is well-documented in contemporary English and Norman chronicles.

events.title: Jin Dynasty consolidates control in northern China
events.description: In 1174, the Jin Dynasty under the Jurchen rulers continued to consolidate its control over northern China, having conquered the Northern Song in 1127. The Jin maintained a dual administrative system incorporating both Jurchen and Han Chinese governance structures. This period saw relative stability in the Jin realm, though the Song Dynasty in the south remained a rival power. The Jin Dynasty would persist until 1234.

sources.name: Jurchen historical records and Chinese chronicles (Shiji tradition)

events.certainty_note: General political stability of the Jin Dynasty in this period is well-attested, though specific events for 1174 are not prominently documented in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Hattin is sometimes portrayed in popular Western accounts as a complete annihilation of Crusader power, but modern scholarship notes that Crusader states persisted for another century (until 1291 in Acre). The battle was devastating but not immediately terminal. Additionally, some sources attribute excessive casualty figures to Hattin; modern estimates are more conservative than medieval chronicles suggest. The significance of Henry II's death in 1174 is sometimes overstated in English historiography; the succession to Richard was not immediately transformative, and Richard did not depart for Crusade until 1191.
historiographic_note: The year 1174 is well-documented for Western Europe and the Levant, with contemporary chronicles from both Christian and Islamic sources. However, documentation is heavily weighted toward political and military events involving elites. Economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. For Asia, sources are primarily from Chinese and Jurchen records, which focus on court and administrative matters. Sub-Saharan Africa and much of Eastern Europe are virtually absent from contemporary written records available to modern historians. The surviving sources reflect the biases of literate elites and religious institutions.

graph_edges.note: Hattin victory enabled Saladin's subsequent conquest of Jerusalem
Richard's accession eventually led to his participation in the Third Crusade
Military victory and political consolidation occurred in parallel
